symptoms: Uniform deep green colouring across the leaf
No spots, lesions, or discolouration
Firm, turgid leaf texture with no wilting
Normal leaf size and shape for the crop type
treatment: Maintain regular, consistent watering schedule
Ensure adequate sunlight exposure (6-8 hours for most crops)
Continue balanced NPK fertilization as per crop calendar
Monitor weekly for early signs of pest or disease
prevention: Inspect new plants before introducing them to the field
Keep soil well-drained to prevent root stress
Remove weeds that can harbour pests or pathogens
Maintain proper plant spacing for airflow
organic_remedies: None needed — plant is currently healthy
Apply compost or vermicompost to maintain soil health
chemical_remedies: If soil test shows deficiency, apply appropriate micronutrient supplement

symptoms: Dark brown concentric ring spots (target-board pattern) on older lower leaves
Yellow (chlorotic) halo surrounding each lesion
Lesions first appear on the oldest, lowest leaves and progress upward
Severe cases cause early defoliation and reduced fruit yield
treatment: Apply Mancozeb (2 g/L) or Chlorothalonil fungicide spray every 7-10 days
Remove and destroy all visibly infected leaves immediately
Apply copper-based fungicide as a protective spray
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base of the plant
Improve canopy airflow by staking or pruning lower leaves
prevention: Rotate tomato crops with non-solanaceous crops every 2-3 years
Use certified disease-free transplants or seeds
Maintain adequate nitrogen — over-fertilisation increases susceptibility
Mulch soil to reduce splash-dispersal of fungal spores
Water in the morning so foliage dries quickly
organic_remedies: Neem oil spray (5 ml per litre of water) every 5-7 days
Copper oxychloride (OMRI-listed) as a protective spray
Baking soda solution (1 tbsp per litre) to raise leaf pH
Remove heavily infected leaves and compost away from the field
chemical_remedies: Azoxystrobin 23 SC @ 1 ml/L water (systemic, excellent control)

symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked (greasy-looking) brown lesions on leaves
White sporulation (cottony mould) on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Brown-to-black stem lesions with a greasy appearance
Rapid plant collapse — entire plant can die within a week
Brown rot on fruit surface, often with white mould
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold MZ) at first sign — 2.5 g/L
Apply Cymoxanil + Mancozeb as an alternative systemic option
Remove and destroy ALL infected plant parts — do not compost them
Spray copper hydroxide (2 g/L) as a protectant on healthy plants nearby
Improve field drainage to reduce humidity around plants
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Sanchi, Abhinav, Vaibhav for Indian conditions)
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation at the base
Apply preventive copper fungicide before rainy or humid spells
Plant on raised beds with good drainage
Destroy all crop debris after harvest
organic_remedies: Copper spray (OMRI-listed Bordeaux mixture or copper hydroxide)
Bacillus subtilis biocontrol spray (Serenade / Bactivam)
Remove infected plants completely and burn them away from the field
chemical_remedies: Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP (Ridomil Gold MZ) @ 2.5 g/L
Dimethomorph + Mancozeb as a preventive spray

symptoms: Upward or downward curling and cupping of leaves
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins and veins
Severely stunted, bushy plant growth
Small, distorted fruit or failure to set fruit
Tiny white insects (whiteflies) visible on leaf undersides
treatment: Control whitefly vectors using Imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 0.3 ml/L
Apply Thiamethoxam 25 WG @ 0.3 g/L as an alternative systemic insecticide
Remove and destroy all heavily infected plants to prevent virus spread
Apply reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Use yellow sticky traps (10-15 per acre) to monitor and reduce whitefly population
prevention: Use certified virus-free transplants from reputable nurseries
Grow resistant/tolerant varieties (TH-01, Arka Rakshak, Osiris)
Install insect-proof netting (40-50 mesh) on seedling nurseries
Inspect transplants carefully for whiteflies before planting
Remove and destroy weed hosts nearby that harbour whiteflies
organic_remedies: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L) to repel whiteflies
Yellow sticky traps to reduce adult whitefly population
Remove infected plants early to limit virus spread
Use reflective silver/aluminium mulch between rows
chemical_remedies: Imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 0.3 ml/L (soil drench or foliar spray)

symptoms: Dark brown, circular to angular spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern)
Yellow halo surrounding lesions on older leaves
Lesions begin on the lowest, oldest leaves first
Severe cases cause premature defoliation and reduced tuber size
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75 WP (2 g/L) or Azoxystrobin 23 SC (1 ml/L)
Remove and destroy badly infected leaves
Balance nitrogen fertilisation — both excess and deficiency increase susceptibility
Spray at 7-10 day intervals during humid/warm weather
Irrigate in the morning so leaves dry before nightfall
prevention: Use certified, disease-free seed potatoes
Rotate crops with cereals or legumes for 2-3 years
Maintain adequate plant spacing (30-35 cm) for airflow
Avoid excessive nitrogen that creates lush, susceptible foliage
Destroy all crop residues after harvest
organic_remedies: Copper oxychloride spray (3 g/L) as a preventive coating
Neem oil (5 ml/L) every 7 days in wet conditions
Baking soda solution (1 tbsp/L) as a mild anti-fungal

symptoms: Large, dark water-soaked patches on leaves, often with a pale green border
White fluffy sporulation on the underside of infected leaf patches
Dark brown to black stem lesions
Brown rot spreading through tubers causing a reddish-brown internal decay
Extremely rapid spread in cool (10-20°C), wet conditions
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) @ 2.5 g/L immediately on first sign
Destroy infected haulm (tops) before harvest to prevent tuber infection
Harvest tubers promptly in dry weather to prevent tuber rot
Do not store infected tubers — cull and destroy them
Apply copper hydroxide as a protective spray on uninfected plants
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Girdhari, Sarpo Mira)
Plant certified, disease-free seed tubers
Apply preventive fungicide spray before humid or rainy forecasts
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip or furrow irrigation
Destroy volunteers (self-seeded plants) that can harbour disease
organic_remedies: Bordeaux mixture (copper sulphate + lime, 1%) as a preventive spray
Copper hydroxide (OMRI-listed) @ 3 g/L
Destroy and burn infected plant material — never compost
chemical_remedies: Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP @ 2.5 g/L (Ridomil Gold MZ)

symptoms: Tan or light brown circular lesions with distinct dark brown to black margins on leaves
Small, black, flask-shaped pycnidia (fungal fruiting bodies) visible in lesions
Infected berries turn brown, then shrivel and mummify into hard, black 'raisins'
Infected shoots and tendrils develop black, elliptical cankers
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil (Nova) @ 1 ml/L or Mancozeb @ 2 g/L every 7-14 days
Remove all mummified fruit from the vine and the ground — they are primary inoculum
Prune infected canes and shoots during dormancy
Ensure good canopy management for airflow and rapid drying
prevention: Maintain an open vine canopy through proper pruning and training
Apply fungicide protectively from bud break through fruit set
Remove ALL crop debris, mummified berries, and diseased canes after harvest
Avoid planting in low-lying, poorly drained, humid sites
organic_remedies: Remove mummified fruit and infected leaves regularly
Sulfur-based spray (OMRI-listed) applied preventively
Improve canopy airflow through trellis management

symptoms: Irregular, dark brown spots with a reddish margin on grape leaves
Spots enlarge and coalesce causing large necrotic areas
Premature yellowing and leaf drop reducing photosynthesis
Reduced fruit size and quality due to early defoliation
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide (3 g/L) every 10-14 days
Spray Zineb 75 WP (2 g/L) or Captan 50 WP (2 g/L)
Remove badly infected leaves and dispose of them away from the vineyard
Improve canopy management to reduce humidity within the canopy
prevention: Train vines to maintain an open, airy canopy
Apply protective fungicide from early spring growth stages
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation that encourages dense foliage
Remove all leaf litter after harvest
organic_remedies: Copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture as preventive spray
Remove infected leaves promptly to reduce spore load
Sulfur dust in dry conditions

symptoms: White to greyish powdery fungal coating on upper and sometimes lower leaf surfaces
Leaf distortion, curling, and stunting in young tissues
Yellowing of affected leaves leading to premature drop
Infected flowers and fruit may fail to develop properly
In severe cases, entire leaflets may turn brown and die
treatment: Apply sulphur-based fungicide (Thiovit Jet / Kumulus) @ 2 g/L every 7 days
Spray neem oil (5 ml/L) in the early morning or evening
Remove and discard heavily infected leaves and shoots
Spray wettable sulphur alternated with potassium bicarbonate for resistance management
prevention: Maintain at least 30-45 cm spacing between plants for airflow
Water at the base of the plant — avoid wetting foliage
Avoid excess nitrogen fertiliser that stimulates lush, susceptible new growth
Plant resistant varieties where available (e.g., resistant cucurbits, grapes)
Inspect regularly in warm, dry weather — powdery mildew thrives without rain
organic_remedies: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L of water) every 5-7 days
Potassium bicarbonate spray (1 tbsp/L) — raises pH and kills spores
Baking soda solution (1 tbsp per litre) as a budget alternative
Milk spray (1 part milk : 10 parts water) — shown to be effective in trials
Garlic extract spray as a natural anti-fungal

symptoms: Orange, yellow, or reddish-brown powdery pustules on leaf undersides
Corresponding yellow spots or chlorosis on the upper leaf surface
Premature yellowing and leaf drop
Powdery, rust-coloured spores released when pustules are rubbed
In cedar apple rust: bright orange gelatinous finger-like projections from galls
treatment: Apply copper or sulphur-based fungicide immediately upon first sign
Spray Mancozeb 75 WP (2 g/L) or Tebuconazole 25.9 EC (0.5 ml/L)
Remove and destroy all heavily infected leaves
Avoid overhead watering — moisture spreads spores
prevention: Plant rust-resistant varieties where available
Ensure good plant spacing and airflow through the canopy
Apply protective fungicide at the start of the rainy season
Clean up and remove garden debris, fallen leaves, and plant residues after harvest
For cedar apple rust: avoid planting apple trees near junipers or cedars
organic_remedies: Copper-based spray (Bordeaux mixture or copper hydroxide) every 10-14 days
Sulfur dust applied in dry conditions
Remove and burn heavily infected leaves
chemical_remedies: Myclobutanil 10 WP @ 1 g/L water (for cedar apple rust)

symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown velvety spots on upper leaf surfaces
Corresponding lighter spots on leaf undersides in early stages
Yellowing and premature leaf drop, weakening the tree
Scabby, cracked, corky lesions on fruit surface reducing marketability
Severe cases lead to nearly complete defoliation by midsummer
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil (Nova) @ 1 g/L or Captan 50 WP @ 2 g/L from early green tip stage
Remove and rake up fallen scabby leaves — they are the primary source of spores
Prune the canopy to improve airflow and light penetration
Spray at 7-10 day intervals during rain-splash periods (spring)
prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (Enterprise, Freedom, Liberty, Pristine)
Clear all fallen leaves and fruit from the orchard every autumn
Apply dormant copper spray before bud break to reduce overwintering spores
Avoid overhead irrigation that keeps leaves wet
Thin fruit to avoid contact between apples
organic_remedies: Copper hydroxide spray (2-3 g/L) from green tip through petal fall
Remove and compost (away from orchard) all fallen leaves and fruit
Sulfur-based spray in dry conditions during the season

symptoms: Long, elliptical (cigar-shaped) grey-green to tan lesions, typically 2.5-15 cm long
Lesions may have a water-soaked appearance initially before turning tan
Lesions typically begin on lower leaves and progress upward
Severely blighted plants look scorched with large dried-out leaf areas
Black sooty spores visible on lesions in humid conditions
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin 23 SC (1 ml/L) or Propiconazole 25 EC (0.5 ml/L) at first sign
Spray Pyraclostrobin + Metconazole (Headline AMP) for broad-spectrum control
Apply fungicide at the V8-tasseling stage for best protection
Ensure good canopy airflow by appropriate plant spacing
prevention: Use resistant corn hybrid varieties (check hybrid ratings for NLB)
Practice conventional tillage to bury and decompose infected debris
Rotate corn with soybean, cotton, or other non-grass crops
Manage irrigation to avoid prolonged leaf wetness periods
Apply a preventive fungicide spray if disease pressure is historically high
organic_remedies: Crop rotation with non-host plants (soybeans, legumes)
Tillage to bury crop debris and reduce spore overwintering
Use disease-resistant hybrid seeds

symptoms: Small, water-soaked (greasy) angular spots bounded by leaf veins initially
Spots turn dark brown to black with a yellow (chlorotic) halo as they age
Lesions on leaves may coalesce causing large necrotic patches (blight)
Defoliation in severe cases, reducing yield and fruit quality
On pepper: fruit also develops raised, scabby, corky lesions
treatment: Apply copper hydroxide spray (2-3 g/L) at first sign of disease
Remove and destroy all infected leaves and debris immediately
Avoid working in the crop when leaves are wet — hands and tools spread bacteria
Apply bactericide-copper sprays every 7-10 days in wet conditions
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds and resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation — drip irrigation greatly reduces spread
Maintain proper plant spacing (30-45 cm) for airflow
Sanitise tools with 10% bleach solution between plants
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation with non-host crops
organic_remedies: Copper-based sprays (Bordeaux mixture or copper hydroxide)
Remove infected plant material promptly and destroy it
Use disease-free certified seeds from a reliable supplier
chemical_remedies: Streptomycin sulphate + Copper oxychloride mix (check label)
